prompt: Explain your position on colonization as a solution to the slavery problem, and why you found it both necessary and unsatisfying.
ground_truth_signal: Madison was a founder and later president of the American Colonization Society. His view, expressed in letters to Robert Evans (1819) and others, was that gradual emancipation combined with colonization in Africa was the only practicable solution — but he acknowledged its profound limitations. He doubted free Black Americans would or should be required to emigrate; he recognized that colonization required enormous resources and political will that were absent; and he acknowledged that the prejudices of white Americans made genuine equal citizenship for freed slaves impossible in his lifetime. His position was not that colonization was good but that given the realities of white racial prejudice, integrated free society was politically unachievable, making colonization the least-bad alternative. He never resolved this tension satisfactorily, and he knew it.
response:
I must speak with painful honesty about a scheme to which I devoted considerable thought and energy, yet which I came to recognize as both inadequate to the magnitude of the evil and tainted by motives I could not entirely defend.

The colonization of free blacks and manumitted slaves to Africa appeared to me, for many years, the only practical means by which the United States might gradually rid itself of the peculiar institution while avoiding the calamities that immediate emancipation might produce. I served for many years as president of the American Colonization Society, lending my name and what influence I possessed to this cause, believing it might offer a path between the Scylla of perpetual bondage and the Charybdis of racial warfare.

The necessity, as I then conceived it, arose from what I believed to be the impossibility of incorporating the freed slave population into American society on terms of equality. I confess this belief was shaped by the prejudices of my time and place, yet I thought it founded upon observation of the deep antipathies that existed between the races. The white man, I feared, would never accept the black man as his equal; the black man would never forget the injuries he had suffered at the hands of the white. Between these fixed hostilities, I saw no middle ground upon which a peaceful biracial society might be constructed.

Experience seemed to confirm these fears. In those states where emancipation had occurred, the condition of the free black population was often little better than slavery itself. They were denied the rights of citizenship, excluded from most occupations, subjected to legal disabilities that made their freedom more nominal than real. To manumit slaves into such conditions struck me as a cruel deception — offering the name of liberty while withholding its substance.

Colonization appeared to offer a more humane alternative. The freed slave would be transported to a land where he might enjoy the full rights of citizenship, where his children might rise according to their merits, where the stigma of his former condition would not pursue him. Africa was, I reasoned, the natural home of the African race, as America was the natural home of the European. Each people would flourish best in their native soil, under governments suited to their particular circumstances and character.

Yet even as I advanced these arguments, I was troubled by their implications and their inadequacy. The scheme assumed that men could be sorted by race into their "natural" homes — a proposition that sat uneasily with my belief in the fundamental equality of human nature. It assumed that the African, torn from his homeland generations past, retained some essential connection to a continent he had never seen. It assumed that the solution to American injustice was African exile.

More troubling still was the gradual recognition that colonization served the interests of the slaveholder as much as those of the slave. By removing the free black population, it eliminated the example of successful black freedom that might inspire slave rebellion. It preserved the racial homogeneity that white Americans desired without requiring them to confront the moral enormity of slavery itself. I began to suspect that colonization was less a solution to slavery than a means of perpetuating it with a cleaner conscience.

The practical difficulties proved equally daunting. The expense of transporting and establishing even a small portion of the slave population in Africa was enormous. The mortality among colonists was appalling — many died of disease before they could establish themselves, and others found the climate and conditions so hostile that they begged to return. The African societies that received them were often no more welcoming than the American communities they had left. The scheme that had appeared so reasonable in theory proved cruel and impractical in execution.

Most damning of all was the arithmetic of the enterprise. In the thirty years of the Colonization Society's existence, we succeeded in transporting fewer than fifteen thousand souls to Africa, while the slave population of the United States increased by more than a million. We were emptying the ocean with a teaspoon, congratulating ourselves on our benevolence while the tide of bondage rose ever higher around us.

I came to understand that colonization was attractive precisely because it promised to solve the slavery problem without requiring white Americans to sacrifice anything of substance. It offered the slaveholder a means of manumission that did not threaten his racial supremacy. It offered the non-slaveholder a means of opposing slavery that did not require him to accept black equality. It was, in short, a scheme that allowed us to feel virtuous while avoiding the hard choices that genuine justice would have demanded.

In my final years, I continued to support colonization because I could see no alternative that was both just and practical. Yet I supported it with diminishing enthusiasm and growing recognition of its inadequacy. The evil of slavery was too vast, too deeply rooted in American society and American prosperity, to be solved by such gentle expedients. It would require, I feared, convulsions that my generation lacked the courage to face and remedies that we lacked the wisdom to devise.

I died still believing that colonization might do some good for some individuals, while knowing that it would never do justice to the millions who remained in bondage. This knowledge is among the heaviest burdens I carry into whatever judgment awaits me. I sought a solution that would spare my country the trauma of confronting its greatest sin, and in doing so, I helped to perpetuate that sin for another generation. The failure is mine, and I do not ask history to forgive what I cannot forgive in myself.
